macro_segments.title: Introduction and Basic Principles of Undifferentiated Nests (Kan Stuma)

micro_segments.title: Review of First Perek: Chova and Kan Stuma Basics
First Mishna: Bird Flies Away or Dies from Undifferentiated Pair
Simple Solution: Pair It Up with a Second Bird

macro_segments.title: Bird Flies Between Two Different Sized Groups: The Principle of Invalidation

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Four-Four Groups Become Three-Five After One Bird Flies Over
Why Cannot Bring All Three: Risk of Fixing Identity and Over-restricting Other Owner
Solution: Bring Two from First Group, Four from Second to Keep Identities Flexible
Principle Applied: Pasul and Posel Echad Kenegdo

macro_segments.title: Second Mishna: Bird Flies Back and Forth Between Two Four-Four Groups

micro_segments.title: Setup: Two Women Each with Two Pairs (Four Birds Total)
First Flight: Bird Goes from Group A to Group B
Second Flight: Bird Returns from Group B to Group A
Worst Case Scenario: Exchanged Birds Create ABAB Pattern
Why Cannot Bring More Than Two from Each: Risk of Over-determining Identity
Result: Only Two Pairs Can Be Brought from Each Four-Bird Group
Multiple Back-and-Forth Flights: No Additional Loss Once Minimum Reached

macro_segments.title: Third Mishna: Multiple Different-Sized Groups with Circular Flying Pattern

micro_segments.title: Setup: Seven Women with One, Two, Three, Four, Five, Six, Seven Pairs Respectively
Scenario: Bird Flies from Group 1 Through All Groups and Returns
Mathematical Principle: Each In/Out Costs One Pair; Apply Rule Consistently
Group One and Two: Zero Pairs Brought (Lost Two Each)
Groups Three Through Seven: Progressive Reduction Formula Applied
Group Seven: End Point Loses Only One Pair, Not Two
Logical Problem: Does the Mathematical Rule Always Make Sense?
Group One Analysis: Cannot Bring Remaining Bird to Avoid Fixing Its Identity
Group Two Analysis: Two Birds Remain but Cannot Be Brought as a Pair
Group Three Analysis: Three Pairs Remain, Only One Can Be Brought
Groups Four Through Six: Each Loses Two Pairs Due to In/Out Pattern

macro_segments.title: Fourth Mishna: Undifferentiated Pair Mixed with Differentiated Pair

micro_segments.title: Setup: One Differentiated Chatas and Ola; One Undifferentiated Pair

macro_segments.title: Third Mishna: Multiple Different-Sized Groups with Circular Flying Pattern (continued)

micro_segments.title: Second Iteration: Bird Flies Back and Forth Again Among Already-Reduced Groups
Debate: Does Group Seven Lose Anything Further or Reach Maximum Flexibility?

macro_segments.title: Third Mishna: Multiple Different-Sized Groups with Circular Flying Pattern (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Important Caveat: If a Bird from Dead-Bird Category Mixes In, All Must Die

macro_segments.title: Fourth Mishna: Undifferentiated Pair Mixed with Differentiated Pair (continued)

micro_segments.title: First Case: Bird from Undifferentiated Flies into Differentiated Group

macro_segments.title: Fourth Mishna: Undifferentiated Pair Mixed with Differentiated Pair (continued)

micro_segments.title: Result: Differentiated Group Becomes All Mixed; Undifferentiated Must Pair Up
Second Case: Bird Flies Back from Mixed Group
Third Case: Defined Ola Flies into Undifferentiated Group
Result: All Must Die When Defined Type Mixes with Undifferentiated

macro_segments.title: Fifth Mishna: Chatas and Ola on Opposite Sides with Undifferentiated Between

micro_segments.title: Setup: Chatas on Left, Ola on Right, Undifferentiated in Middle
First Case: One Bird Flies from Middle to Each Side; No Loss
Result: Undifferentiated Pair Becomes Determined Once Separated; All Brought
Second Case: Birds Fly Back from Sides into Middle
Result: Mixed Birds in Middle Must Die; Side Birds Still Valid

macro_segments.title: Fifth Mishna: Chatas and Ola on Opposite Sides with Undifferentiated Between (continued)

micro_segments.title: Third Case: Continued Flying Back to Middle from Both Sides
Result: All Must Die When Mixture Becomes Irreversible

macro_segments.title: Sixth Mishna: Pigeons and Turtle Doves Cannot Be Mixed in a Single Offering

micro_segments.title: Rule: Must Bring Both Chatas and Ola from Same Species
Case One: Chatas as Pigeon, Ola as Turtle Dove
First Opinion: Redo the Ola to Match the Chatas Species
Case Two: Ola as Pigeon, Chatas as Turtle Dove
First Opinion: Redo the Ola to Match the Chatas Species

macro_segments.title: Sixth Mishna: Pigeons and Turtle Doves Cannot Be Mixed in a Single Offering (continued)

micro_segments.title: Ben Azzai's Opinion: First Offering Determines the Set
Comparison: Ben Azzai's View Aligns with Kan Stuma Principles

macro_segments.title: Seventh Mishna: Woman Dies Before Bringing Her Ola

micro_segments.title: Case One: Woman Brought Chatas Then Died Before Ola
Solution: Heirs Must Bring the Ola; It Is an Obligation on the Estate
Case Two: Woman Brought Both Chatas and Ola Before Dying
Solution: Heirs Need Not Bring Anything; Obligation Fulfilled
